---
title: "Polynomials"
description: "Unique coefficients and the division algorithm, zeros and factorisation, the fundamental theorem of algebra, and factorisation over ℂ and ℝ — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR Chapter 4."
date: "2026-04-30"
date-modified: "2026-06-23"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Chapter 4 of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

For this chapter, we assume that $\mb{F}$ is $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ (although we have not considered alternatives anyway).

Recall that a map $p:\mb{F}\to\mb{F}$ is a **polynomial** (an element of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$) if its action can be described as

$$p(z)=a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n$$

for all $z\in\mb{F}$, where $a_0,\cdots,a_n$ are some elements in $\mb{F}$.

## Non-subspaces of polynomials
:::{#thm-degree-m-not-subspace}
The subset of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$ given by

$$\{0\}\cup\{p\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F}):\deg p=m\}$$

is not a subspace of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The subset is not closed under addition. A simple example is given by

$$(x^2+x)-x^2=x.$$

</details>

:::{#thm-even-degree-not-subspace}
By the same reasoning,

$$\{0\}\cup\{p\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F}):\deg p\text{ is even}\}$$

is not a subspace of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$.
:::

## Two uniqueness results for polynomials
#### Coefficients
:::{#thm-coefficients-unique}
Each polynomial is uniquely specified by the coefficients. In other words, two polynomials with different coefficients are always distinct.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Similarly to the decomposition of a vector in a direct sum or a linearly independent set, we only have to check that the zero polynomial is uniquely representable.

Equivalently, we can show that a polynomial with a non-zero coefficient is never zero. The intuition is that the graph of any non-zero polynomial diverges to infinity as the argument becomes large, because the leading term ends up dominating.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let us first show that

$$0=a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n$$

implies $a_0=\cdots=a_n=0$. To do so, we will prove the contrapositive.

Suppose that there is at least one non-zero coefficient, and let $m$ be the largest index such that $a_m\neq 0$. We are done if we show the existence of $z$ such that

$$|a_mz^m|>|a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_{m-1}z^{m-1}|,$$

because that implies

$$-a_mz^m\neq a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_{m-1}z^{m-1}\implies a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_mz^m\neq0.$$

To do so, note that for $z>1$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
|a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_{m-1}z^{m-1}|&\leq|a_0|+|a_1z|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}z^{m-1}|\\
&=(|a_0\frac{1}{z^{m-1}}|+|a_1\frac{1}{z^{m-2}}|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}|)z^{m-1}\\
&<(|a_0|+|a_1|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}|)z^{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
$$

So we are looking for $z>1$ such that

$$|a_m|z^m\geq (|a_0|+|a_1|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}|)z^{m-1}\implies z\geq\frac{|a_0|+|a_1|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}|}{|a_m|}.$$

The two requirements can be satisfied if we set

$$z=\frac{|a_0|+|a_1|+\cdots+|a_{m-1}|}{|a_m|}+1.$$

Therefore, $a_m\neq0$ for some $m\leq n$ implies that

$$a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n\neq 0.$$

By contraposition, if $a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n=0$, then we have $a_0=a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$.

Now, suppose that there are two polynomials such that

$$a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n=a'_0+a'_1z+\cdots+a'_nz^n$$

for all $z\in\mb{F}$. This means that

$$(a_0-a'_0)+(a_1-a'_1)z+\cdots+(a_n-a'_n)z^n=0,$$

so our result shows that the two polynomials are identical: $a_i=a'_i$ for all $i=0,\cdots,n$.

</details>

#### Quotient and remainder
:::{#thm-division-algorithm}
For any $p,d\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$ ($d$ for *denominator*), there exist unique $q,r\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$ ($q$ for *quotient*, $r$ for *remainder*) with $\deg r<\deg d$ such that $p=qd+r$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
We can show that the map $T(q,r)=qd+r$, where $q$ and $r$ are polynomials of appropriate degrees, is injective and surjective on the space of all polynomials of degree $p$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $\deg p<\deg d$, we see that the desired polynomials are $q=0$ and $r=p$.

So suppose that $\deg p\geq \deg d$. When we multiply polynomials of degrees $m$ and $n$, we obtain a polynomial of degree $m+n$. So to obtain a polynomial of $\deg p$ through $p=qd+r$ where $\deg r<\deg d$, we see that $q$ has to be a polynomial of degree $\deg p-\deg d$.

So consider the map 

$$T:\mathcal{P}_{\deg p-\deg d}(\mb{F})\times\mathcal{P}_{\deg d-1}(\mb{F})\to\mathcal{P}_{\deg p}(\mb{F})$$ 

defined as $T(q,r)=qd+r$. This is a valid map because $\deg(qd+r)\leq\deg p$. It is trivial to check that $T$ is a linear map.

To establish the sought existence and uniqueness, we need to prove that $T$ is surjective and injective, respectively. Since $\deg qd$ is always at least $\deg d$ as long as $q\neq 0$ (while $\deg r<\deg d$), if we have $qd+r=0$, then $qd=r=0$ for otherwise $qd=-r$ is not possible. In other words, $\ker T=\{0\}$ and so $T$ is injective.

Because of how the dimension of a product of vector spaces works, the dimension of the domain of $T$ is given by

$$\dim(\mathcal{P}_{\deg p-\deg d}(\mb{F}))+\dim(\mathcal{P}_{\deg d-1}(\mb{F}))=(\deg p-\deg d+1)+\deg d=\deg p+1.$$

As $T$ is injective, the rank-nullity theorem shows this to be equal to the dimension of $T$'s image. But since

$$\dim \mathcal{P}_{\deg p}(\mb{F})=\deg p+1,$$

we see that $T$ is surjective, as required.

Therefore, the decomposition $p=qd+r$ is unique when $r$ is restricted to $\deg r<\deg d$.

</details>

This is called the **division algorithm**, although we are not interested in the algorithm itself, but rather just in the existence and uniqueness statements that underlie it.

:::{#exm-quotient-by-multiples}
Let $p$ be a non-zero polynomial of degree $n$, and define the subspace $U=\{pq:q\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})\}$ of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$.

Let $r_s$ denote the remainder of $s$ when "divided" by $p$, so that $\deg r_s<\deg p$. Let us define a map $T:\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})/U\to \mathcal{P}_{n-1}(\mb{F})$ as $T(s+U)=r_s$. Through the division algorithm, we may check that it is well-defined and linear.

For any $r\in\mathcal{P}_{n-1}(\mb{F})$, we have $T(r+U)=r$ and so $T$ is surjective. On the other hand, if we have $r_s=0$, then $s$ is of the form $pq$ and so $s\in U$. This implies that $\ker T=U$, which means that $T$ is injective.

Since $T$ is an isomorphism, we have

$$\dim(\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})/U)=\dim\mathcal{P}_{n-1}(\mb{F})=\deg p.$$

Because the set $1+U,x+U,\cdots,x^{n-1}+U$ spans $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})/U$ and is of the right size, it is a basis of $\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})/U$.
:::

## Zeros of polynomial
A **zero** of a polynomial $p\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$ is an element $\lambda\in\mb{F}$ such that $p(\lambda)=0$.

#### Zero and factorisation
A polynomial $s$ is a **factor** of another polynomial $p$ if there is a polynomial $q$ such that $p=sq$.

:::{#thm-zero-iff-factor}
$\lambda$ is a zero of $p$ if and only if $z-\lambda$ is a factor of $p$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
For the converse direction, use the division algorithm to extract $z-\lambda$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $z-\lambda$ is a factor of $p$, then

$$p(z)=(z-\lambda)s(z)$$

for some polynomial $s$ and so $p(\lambda)=0$.

Conversely, suppose that $p(\lambda)=0$ holds. By the division algorithm, we know there exist a unique pair of polynomial $q$ and constant polynomial $r$ such that

$$p(z)=(z-\lambda)q(z)+r.$$

We just have to show that $r=0$, but this is the case because

$$0=p(\lambda)=0q(\lambda)+r=r.$$

</details>

#### Maximum number of zeros
:::{#thm-max-zeros}
A non-constant polynomial $p$ has at most $\deg p$ zeros.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Prove by induction on the degree of $p$. For the inductive step, use the previous result.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

We will proceed by induction on the degree of $p$.

Suppose that $\deg p=1$. Then $p(z)=a_0+a_1z$ where $a_1\neq0$. If $z$ is a zero of $p$, then

$$0=a_0+a_1z\implies z=-\frac{a_0}{a_1}.$$

Therefore, $p$ has a single zero located at $-a_0/a_1$.

Assume that a polynomial of degree $k-1$ has at most $k-1$ zeroes, and let $p$ be a polynomial of degree $k$. If $\lambda$ is a zero of $p$, then we know that $p$ can be factorised as

$$p(z)=(z-\lambda)q(z)$$

where $q$ is a polynomial of degree $k-1$ and thereby has $k-1$ zeros.

Suppose that $p(z)=0$. Due to the zero-product property, this implies that either $z-\lambda=0$ or $q(z)=0$. The first possibility gives a single zero, while the second gives at most $k-1$ zeros. So $p$ has at most $k$ zeros.

By the principle of induction, the property holds for polynomials of arbitrary degree.

</details>

###### Polynomial of degree $n$ is specifiable by $n+1$ points
:::{#thm-points-determine-polynomial}
Suppose that $z_1,\cdots,z_{n+1}\in\mb{F}$ are distinct, and let $w_1,\cdots,w_{n+1}\in\mb{F}$. Then there is a unique polynomial $p\in\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$ such that $p(z_i)=w_i$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Show that the map between polynomials of degree $n$ and its evaluations at $n+1$ points is injective and surjective.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Consider the evaluation map $T:\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})\to\mb{F}^{n+1}$ given by

$$Tp=(p(z_1),\cdots,p(z_{n+1})).$$

Suppose that $p(z_1)=\cdots=p(z_{n+1})=0$, which means that $z_1,\cdots,z_{n+1}$ are zeros of $p$. But since a non-constant polynomial of degree $n$ has at most $n$ zeroes, this means that $p$ is the zero polynomial. Therefore, we have $\ker T=\{0\}$. 

By the rank-nullity theorem, this implies

$$\dim\im\ T=\dim\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})-\dim\ker T=n+1.$$

Since $\dim\mb{F}^{n+1}=n+1$, this shows $T$ to be surjective.

The injectivity and surjectivity of $T$ shows that, for any $w_1,\cdots,w_{n+1}$, there is a unique polynomial of degree $n$ that evaluates to these values at $z_1,\cdots,z_{n+1}$.

</details>

## Fundamental theorem of algebra
:::{#thm-fundamental-theorem-algebra}
The **fundamental theorem of algebra** states that every non-constant polynomial in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$ has at least one zero.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
We will not prove it here because it requires the use of analysis.
:::

Note that this statement is not true when restricted to real numbers: for example, $p(x)=x^2+1$ has no real zeros.

## Factorising polynomials in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$
:::{#thm-factorisation-complex}
Every non-constant polynomial $p$ in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$ can be uniquely factorised as a product of degree-1 polynomials as

$$
p(z)=c(z-\lambda_1)\cdots(z-\lambda_n)
$$

where $c,\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mathbb{C}$.
:::

:::{.informal}
Any complex polynomial can be "fully factorised" in terms of its zeros.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Show existence by induction on the degree of $p$, then show uniqueness by the zero-product property.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

We will first show the existence of such factorisation by induction. For $\deg p=1$, the assertion is trivially true.

Assume that every polynomial of degree $k-1$ can be factorised in the required manner. Consider an arbitrary polynomial $p$ of degree $k$. By the fundamental theorem of algebra, it has a zero at some location $\lambda$. Then $p$ can be factorised as

$$p(z)=(z-\lambda)q(z)$$

where $q$ has degree $k-1$. But this implies that $p$ can be factorised in the required manner, along with $q$.

By the principle of induction, this factorisation property holds for polynomials of arbitrary degree.

Now suppose that there are two ways to factorise a polynomial $p$ in this form, such that

$$c(z-\lambda_1)\cdots(z-\lambda_n)=c(z-\tau_1)\cdots(z-\tau_n)$$

where $n$ is the degree of $p$. The coefficient $c$ has to be the same on both sides, because that is equal to the coefficient of the leading term. 

From the left expression, we see that $\lambda_1$ is a zero of $p$. Then one of the $\tau_i$'s must be equal to $\lambda_1$, for otherwise the product will not be zero. In this manner, every $\lambda_i$ must correspond to a $\tau_i$. 

By the same argument, every $\tau_i$ must correspond to a $\lambda_i$. Therefore, the $\lambda_i$s and the $\tau_i$s must match one-to-one, and the two expressions must be equal. So the expression of a polynomial in this factorised manner must be unique.

</details>

#### Constructing polynomials with particular zeros
We have seen that a polynomial of degree $n$ can have at most $n$ zeros. As it turns out, for any $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_m\in\mb{F}$ where $m\leq n$, there exists a polynomial of degree $n$ whose zeros are exactly located at $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_m$ and no more. Here is an example:

$$(z-\lambda_1)^{n-m+1}(z-\lambda_2)\cdots(z-\lambda_m).$$

#### Polynomials with real coefficients
:::{#thm-real-coeff-conjugate-zeros}
Suppose that $p$ is a polynomial in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$ whose coefficients are all real. Then its zeros come in pairs: if $\lambda$ is a zero, then so is $\overline{\lambda}$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider the complex conjugate of the entire equation: $\overline{p(\lambda)}=0$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Consider a polynomial

$$p(z)=a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n$$

where $a_i\in\mathbb{R}$, and let $\lambda\in\mathbb{C}$ be a zero of $p$. Then we have

$$
\begin{gather}
0=a_0+a_1\lambda+\cdots+a_n\lambda^n\\
\implies 0=a_0+a_1\overline{\lambda}+\cdots+a_n\overline{\lambda^n}=a_0+a_1\overline{\lambda}+\cdots+a_n\overline{\lambda}^n,
\end{gather}
$$

where we use the property $\overline{zw}=\overline{z}\ \overline{w}$. Therefore, we see that $\overline{\lambda}$ is also a zero of $p$.

</details>

## Factorising polynomials in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$
#### Quadratic polynomial
:::{#thm-quadratic-factorisation}
Let $b,c\in\mathbb{R}$. Then there exist $\lambda_1,\lambda_2\in\mathbb{R}$ such that

$$x^2+bx+c=(x-\lambda_1)(x-\lambda_2)$$

if and only if $b^2\geq4c$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
For the converse direction, complete the square to force $x^2+bx+c$ into the desired form.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that there exist $\lambda_1,\lambda_2\in\mathbb{R}$ such that

$$x^2+bx+c=(x-\lambda_1)(x-\lambda_2)=x^2-(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)x+\lambda_1\lambda_2.$$

Then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
b^2-4c&=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2-4\lambda_1\lambda_2\\
&=\lambda_1^2-2\lambda_1\lambda_2+\lambda_2^2\\
&=(\lambda_1-\lambda_2)^2\\
&\geq0
\end{aligned}
$$

as required. 

Conversely, suppose that $b^2\geq 4c$. Note that completing the square gives us

$$x^2+bx+c=(x+\frac{b}{2})^2+c-\frac{b^2}{4}.$$

As the initial inequality leads to $b^2/4-c\geq 0$, we can continue as

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2+bx+c&=(x+\frac{b}{2})^2-(\frac{b^2}{4}-c)\\
&=(x+\frac{b}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{b^2}{4}-c})(x+\frac{b}{2}+\sqrt{\frac{b^2}{4}-c}).
\end{aligned}
$$

So we have the desired factorisation with

$$\lambda_1=\frac{b}{2}+\sqrt{\frac{b^2}{4}-c}\quad\text{and}\quad\lambda_2=\frac{b}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{b^2}{4}-c},$$

where both are real.

</details>

#### General polynomial
:::{#thm-factorisation-real}
A non-constant polynomial $p\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ has a unique factorisation of the form

$$p(x)=c(x-\lambda_1)\cdots(x-\lambda_n)(x^2+b_1x+c_1)\cdots(x^2+b_mx+c_m)$$

where $c,\lambda_i,b_i,c_i\in\mathbb{R}$ and $b_i^2< 4c_i$ for all $i$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
At each step of the factorisation, either the zero is real (leading to a quadratic factor) or non-real complex (leading to a linear factor).

But after each step, we should ensure that the as-yet-unfactored polynomial still has real coefficients, which can be derived after showing that it always takes on real values.

The uniqueness of the factorisation is a consequence of the analogous uniqueness for $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let us view $p$ as a polynomial in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$. First, suppose that the zeros $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_k$ of $p$ are all real. Then $p$ can be uniquely factored as

$$p(z)=c(z-\lambda_1)\cdots(z-\lambda_k)$$

by the unique factorisation of polynomials in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$, and we are done. 

So suppose that $p$ has a non-real zero $\lambda$, in which case we know that $\overline{\lambda}$ is also a zero of $p$. Subsequently, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x)&=(x-\lambda)(x-\overline{\lambda})q(x)\\
&=(x^2-(\lambda+\overline{\lambda})x+\lambda\overline{\lambda})q(x)\\
&=(x^2-2(\text{Re}\ \lambda)x+|\lambda|^2)q(x),
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\deg q=\deg p-2$. Because $|\lambda|^2>(\text{Re}\ \lambda)^2$, we have

$$4|\lambda|^2>4(\text{Re}\ \lambda)^2=(2(\text{Re}\ \lambda))^2$$

and so the quadratic factor in the above expression satisfies our requirement.

We may then factor out $(x-\lambda')(x-\overline{\lambda'})$ from $q(x)$ if $q(x)$ has a non-real zero, or otherwise completely factorise $q$ into the factors of real zeros. If the former is the case, we can repeat this process.

If the process does not terminate (by obtaining a polynomial with real zeros only), we will either end up with a degree-1 or degree-2 polynomial $q'(x)$. If we have the former---with $q'(x)=x-a$---we obtain a real zero $a$ from this polynomial. If we have the latter---with $q'(x)=x^2+bx+c$---either $b^2\geq 4c$ and we obtain two real roots, or we simply append $x^2+bx+c$ to the factorisation.

However, for this argument to go through, the coefficients of $q(x)$ must be real. To verify this, note that the value of $q(x)$ is given by

$$q(x)=\frac{p(x)}{x^2-2(\text{Re}\ \lambda)x+|\lambda|^2},$$ 

for all $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Since both the numerator and denominator are always real, so is the value of $q(x)$.

The real-ness of $q(x)$ implies

$$0=\text{Im}\ q(x)=\text{Im}\ a_0+(\text{Im}\ a_1)x+\cdots+(\text{Im}\ a_{n-2})x^{n-2}.$$

We know that a zero polynomial like this is only possible with $\text{Im}\ a_0=\cdots=\text{Im}\ a_{n-2}=0$. Therefore, the coefficients of $q(x)$ are real, and the sought factorisation does exist.

To show that this factorisation is unique, we only need to note that the quadratic polynomials can be further factorised if we are working in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$. So if this factorisation is not unique, the uniqueness of the degree-1 factorisation for polynomials in $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$ will be violated. 

</details>

:::{#thm-odd-degree-real-zero}
By looking at the above factorisation process, we can see that polynomials of odd order always have at least one real zero. 
:::

## Obtaining polynomials with real coefficients
#### From complex conjugate
:::{#thm-conjugate-product-real}
For any $p\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{C})$, the polynomial

$$q(z)=p(z)\overline{p(\overline{z})}$$

has real coefficients. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

This is because

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z)\overline{p(\overline{z})}&=(a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n)\overline{(a_0+a_1\overline{z}+\cdots+a_n\overline{z^n})}\\
&=(a_0+a_1z+\cdots+a_nz^n)(\overline{a_0}+\overline{a_1}z+\cdots+\overline{a_n}z^n)\\
&=\sum_{i=0}^n\sum_{j=0}^n(a_iz^i)(\overline{a_j}z^j)\\
&=\sum_{k=0}^n|a_k|^2z^{2k}+\sum_{j\neq i}a_i\overline{a_j}z^{i+j}\\
&=\sum_{k=0}^n|a_k|^2z^{2k}+\sum_{j>i}(a_i\overline{a_j}+a_j\overline{a_i})z^{i+j}\\
&=\sum_{k=0}^n|a_k|^2z^{2k}+\sum_{j>i}2\text{Re}(a_i\overline{a_j})z^{i+j}.
\end{aligned}
$$

</details>

#### From real evaluations at real points
:::{#thm-real-evaluations-real-coeff}
Suppose that $p\in\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is such that $p(x_j)\in\mathbb{R}$ for $n+1$ distinct points $x_0,\cdots,x_n\in\mathbb{R}$. Then the coefficients of $p$ are real.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This is corollary of "$n+1$ points determine a polynomial of degree $n$".
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Previously, we showed that the evaluation map $T:\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})\to\mb{F}^{n+1}$ defined as

$$Tp=(p(z_1),\cdots,p(z_{n+1}))$$

is an isomorphism. 

If we take $\mb{F}=\mathbb{R}$, we see that there is a unique $p\in\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})$ that goes through $p(x_0),\cdots,p(x_n)$. Since we have $\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})\subseteq \mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{C})$, this $p$ must also be the unique polynomial of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{C})$ that goes through these points. 

</details>
